ontology.foodex2_description: Wonton soup — composite dish of filled dumplings in broth
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No exact FoodEx2 code for shrimp-pork wonton soup; A0FKP (filled pasta in broth) used as closest defensible analogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept identified for wonton soup at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of shrimp and pork wonton soup served in Singapore hawker centres typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising 6–8 wontons in superior chicken broth.

nutrient_source.title: Wonton Soup (Shrimp and Pork) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for 虾肉馄饨汤 (Shrimp & Pork Wantons Served In Superior Chicken Broth), cr_id G00010065. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported in the HPB entry are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for shrimp and pork wonton soup. The HPB-reported carbohydrate for this dish is 0.0 g/100g, which likely reflects the filling only and does not capture the wonton wrapper contribution. A GI of 55 is considered low based on University of Sydney GI Database guidelines (GI <= 55 is low). This is estimated based on the closest analogous food: wheat-based filled pasta/dumplings in broth, consistent with University of Sydney GI Database entries for boiled wheat dumplings and wonton-type products. Glycemic load per serving is calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100; because the HPB-reported carbohydrate is 0.0 g/100g for this record, GL per serving resolves to 0.0. Consumers should note that wonton wrappers do contribute refined carbohydrates not fully captured in this HPB entry.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 1050 mg/100g — far exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; sodium at 1050 mg/100g is 175% of the 600 mg/100g upper limit, and consuming a standard 350 g bowl delivers approximately 3675 mg sodium, well above the 2000 mg daily target — request reduced-sodium broth and consume only a small portion if at all.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Wonton wrappers contribute refined carbohydrates not fully captured in the HPB carbohydrate figure of 0.0 g/100g
type2_diabetes.guidance: The HPB-recorded carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g likely reflects the filling only; wonton wrappers are made from refined wheat flour and will raise postprandial blood glucose — individuals with type 2 diabetes should monitor portion size, limit the number of wontons consumed, and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate glycaemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) and moderate fat (10.5 g/100g) with cholesterol at 89 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The very high sodium content (1050 mg/100g) poses a direct cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation, and cholesterol at 89 mg/100g combined with pork-derived saturated fat warrants caution — individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request low-sodium broth, and consume this dish only occasionally as part of a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (1050 mg/100g) and pork-derived arachidonic acid (omega-6) amplify inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Sodium at 1050 mg/100g worsens tissue oedema and inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis, while pork filling contributes arachidonic acid that amplifies inflammatory cytokine activity — individuals with RA should request reduced-sodium broth, consider substituting pork with shrimp-only or tofu filling, and limit portion size significantly.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shrimp and Pork Wonton Soup in Superior Chicken Broth (虾肉馄饨汤) is a classic Chinese soup dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and Chinese restaurants. It consists of hand-folded wontons filled with a mixture of minced shrimp and pork, wrapped in thin wheat-flour skins and boiled until tender, then served in a rich, slow-simmered superior chicken broth seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, and white pepper. The wontons have a delicate, slightly chewy skin with a juicy, savoury filling that delivers a clean umami flavour. The broth is clear, aromatic, and deeply savoury. Per 100 g, the HPB Singapore database records 171 kcal, 19.1 g protein, 10.5 g fat, 0.0 g carbohydrate (note: this reflects the filling; wonton wrappers contribute additional refined carbohydrates), and 1050 mg sodium. The very high sodium content (1050 mg/100g) makes this dish a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease. A standard 350 g bowl delivers approximately 3675 mg sodium, exceeding the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg. The dish is protein-rich, supporting satiety and muscle maintenance, but the high sodium and cholesterol (89 mg/100g) require moderation for at-risk individuals. Clinically, this dish is not suitable for hypertension or cardiovascular disease without significant sodium reduction, and individuals with type 2 diabetes should account for the carbohydrate contribution of the wonton wrappers despite the 0.0 g/100g HPB figure.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010065), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. The record is marked as reviewed with confidence 0.95 in the source system. The carbohydrate value of 0.0 g/100g is noted as a known limitation of the HPB entry for this dish type.
notes: carbohydrate_g is reported as 0.0 g/100g by HPB Singapore for this dish; this is a known data limitation — the HPB entry appears to reflect the filling composition only and does not fully capture the refined carbohydrate contribution of the wonton wheat-flour wrappers. This is confirmed zero per HPB source but is not naturally zero for the complete dish. sugar_g is 0.0 g/100g per HPB — confirmed zero per HPB source. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 g/100g per HPB — confirmed zero per HPB source. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this dish as of 2026. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for this dish. GI of 55 is an analogous estimate based on boiled wheat-based filled dumplings from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific dish. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0 based on the HPB-reported carbohydrate of 0.0 g/100g; actual GL will be higher when wonton wrapper carbohydrate is accounted for.
